title: The Ugly Duckling
content:
It was lovely summer weather in the country, and the golden corn, the green oats, and the haystacks piled up in the meadows looked beautiful.

A duck sat on her nest, waiting for her eggs to hatch. At last one shell cracked, then another.

"Peep, peep!" said the little ones as they came to life.

But the biggest egg hadn't hatched yet. "That must be a turkey's egg," said an old duck who had come to visit. "Leave it alone."

"I'll sit on it a little longer," said the mother duck.

At last the great egg broke, and a young one crept out. It was very large and ugly.

"That is a terribly large duckling," said the old duck. "None of the others look like that."

The next day the mother duck took her family to the water. Splash! She jumped in, and one after another, the little ducklings jumped in too.

The ugly duckling swam about with the others, but everyone pecked at him and made fun of him.

"You are too ugly," they said. Even his brothers and sisters were unkind to him.

The poor duckling was very sad. He ran away from the farmyard and hid among the reeds by a great lake.

Winter came, and it was very cold. The duckling had to swim round and round in the water to keep from freezing.

At last spring arrived. The duckling flapped his wings. They were much stronger now. He flew to a beautiful garden where three elegant white swans swam in a pond.

"I will go to those royal birds," he said. "They will kill me because I am so ugly. But it is better to be killed by them than to be bitten by the ducks."

He flew to the water and swam towards the beautiful swans. They came to meet him with outstretched wings.

"Kill me," said the poor bird, and bent his head down to the surface of the water, waiting for death.

But what did he see in the clear water? His own image! He was no longer a dark, gray bird, ugly and disagreeable to look at. He was himself a swan!

The other swans swam round him and stroked his neck with their beaks.

Some little children came to the garden with bread and corn to throw to the swans.

"Look, there is a new one!" cried the youngest. "He is the most beautiful of all!"

The young swan felt quite shy and hid his head under his wing. He had been so unhappy, and now he was the happiest of all.

He thought of how he had been teased, and now he heard everyone say he was the most beautiful of all beautiful birds.

He rustled his feathers, curved his slender neck, and cried joyfully, "I never dreamed of such happiness when I was an ugly duckling."

The End.

author: Hans Christian Andersen
